narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 47% chance of not passing. Picture the model as an experienced advisor who's reviewed thousands of stories like yours, and it zeroed in first on your LSAT score of 32, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward potential struggles on the bar, more than anything else in your profile. Then came your Year 3 decile of 1.0, showing you were at the lower end of your school's rankings that year, which reinforced that concern and painted a picture of mounting academic hurdles. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 echoed this early on, suggesting those challenges started right away and didn't turn around. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model linked to added pressures that have tripped up others in similar spots. Your Asian background and undergraduate GPA of 2.7 also nudged the outlook toward failure in the model's eyes, as patterns from past students with those traits showed tougher odds. Even your gender as female and full-time study plan tilted things that way overall, though going full-time was the one bright spot, gently pulling toward success amid the rest. Together, these pieces formed a story of steady underperformance against the benchmarks that usually light the way to bar passage, like stronger test scores and higher deciles. It's not that your determination or any single thing dooms you—far from it—but the combination just didn't align with the profiles that reliably cross the finish line. This isn't the end of your journey; many have boosted low starts with targeted prep, and you could too. Hang in there—you've got the drive, and a little strategic pivot might rewrite this story.
